key_requirements: ASEAN working toward mutual recognition for PPE including protective helmets; ACCSQ PPE Working Group; alignment with international standards (IEC/ISO); national certifications prevail for cycling helmets (each country: ID=SNI, TH=TIS, VN=QCVN, SG=SS, MY=JKR/SIRIM, PH=PNS); no single ASEAN cycling helmet standard yet; ACCSQ promotes harmonisation
notes: ASEAN MRA framework exists but not fully implemented for cycling helmets; each country maintains own certification; exporters to ASEAN must obtain country-by-country certification; ACCSQ working toward convergence with ISO/IEC standards; medium-term harmonisation expected

key_requirements: GSO 1798 covers protective helmets applicable to cycling/motorcycle use in GCC countries; SASO type approval for Saudi market; ESMA registration for UAE; GSO conformity mark; testing by GSO-approved lab; Arabic labelling; country of origin; manufacturer details; shock absorption and retention tests per GSO/ISO; mandatory for helmets sold in GCC
notes: GCC: GSO 1798 reference standard; SASO type approval most stringent (Saudi Arabia); UAE: ESMA registration; Qatar: QSTP; Bahrain/Kuwait/Oman: GSO conformity; ISO 6220 and EN 1078 referenced; cycling helmet mandatory use: limited regulation in GCC (cultural/climate factors); primary requirement = product compliance

regulation_ref: Israeli Standard SI 2761 (מגיני ראש לרוכבי אופניים)
enforcement_body: SII (Standards Institution of Israel) + Ministry of Economy
key_requirements: SI 2761 is mandatory Israeli standard for bicycle helmets; SII certification required; Israeli Mandatory Standard (תקן ישראלי רשמי); shock absorption; retention system; area of protection; labelling in Hebrew; Road Traffic Ordinance: mandatory helmet use for cyclists < 18 years in Israel; SII Mark required for sale; testing by SII-accredited lab
notes: Israel: SI 2761 mandatory standard; SII certification = mandatory for sale; Road Traffic Ordinance: mandatory <18 years (adults: recommended not mandatory); Hebrew labelling required; SII certification process: technical documentation + sample testing + factory audit; CE not automatically accepted

enforcement_body: Egyptian Organization for Standardization and Quality (EOS) + GOEIC
key_requirements: Egyptian Standard ES 4768 references for bicycle helmets; GOEIC pre-shipment inspection for PPE imports; Egyptian standards aligned with ISO/EN basis; conformity certificate from EOS-accredited body; Arabic labelling required; import customs documentation; GOEIC clearance mandatory for regulated product imports
notes: Egypt: ES 4768 for cycling helmets; GOEIC mandatory pre-shipment inspection for imports; EOS oversees standards; Arabic labelling required; CE or ISO-compliant helmets may be accepted if GOEIC inspection passed; no specific mandatory cycling helmet use law nationally

regulation_name: Décret n°2-09-689 — Sécurité des produits + NM standards
regulation_ref: Décret n°2-09-689 relatif à la sécurité des produits et services (Maroc)
key_requirements: Loi-cadre sécurité des produits au Maroc; normes marocaines NM pour équipements de protection; IMANOR publie normes NM alignées avec ISO/EN; casques vélo: NM référencée ou équivalence EN 1078 acceptée; marquage CE peut faciliter l'entrée; étiquetage français/arabe; importateurs responsables de la conformité
notes: Maroc: cadre sécurité produits via Décret 2-09-689; IMANOR gère normes NM; alignement progressif avec EN/ISO; EN 1078 CE peut faciliter admission; port du casque: obligation légale inexistante à l'échelle nationale (recommandation); importateurs: responsabilité conformité

regulation_ref: SANS 1066 (South African National Standard — Protective helmets for cyclists)
enforcement_body: SABS (South African Bureau of Standards) + NRCS (National Regulator for Compulsory Specifications)
key_requirements: SANS 1066 is the South African standard for protective helmets for cyclists; NRCS may issue Compulsory Specification (VC) for helmets; SABS Mark Scheme; shock absorption; retention system; labelling; Road Traffic Act: no national mandatory helmet law for cyclists currently; NRCS Letter of Authority (LoA) if VC issued; importers must demonstrate conformity
notes: ZA: SANS 1066 aligned with ISO/EN 1078; NRCS has powers to make VC mandatory; Road Traffic Act: currently no national mandatory helmet use law for cyclists (adults); some municipal bylaws may differ; SABS certification recommended; importers: NRCS registration for PPE products

regulation_ref: SON (Standards Organisation of Nigeria) + SONCAP for imports
key_requirements: SON manages Nigerian Industrial Standards (NIS) including protective helmets; SONCAP (SON Conformity Assessment Programme) mandatory for imported helmet products; NIS standards for protective equipment; SON Mark for domestically produced helmets; Road Traffic Act: motorcycle helmet mandatory (not explicitly cycling helmets in most states); SONCAP inspection at origin
notes: Nigeria: SONCAP mandatory for PPE imports from non-ECOWAS countries; SON NIS standards for protective helmets; motorcycle helmets = strictly enforced; cycling helmet: growing awareness but limited enforcement; SONCAP certificate required at customs clearance

regulation_ref: Kenya Bureau of Standards (KEBS) + KS 1888-1 (Cyclists' protective helmets)
key_requirements: KEBS PVoC (Pre-export Verification of Conformity) mandatory for regulated products including PPE/protective helmets; KS 1888-1 is the Kenyan standard for cyclists' protective helmets; KEBS inspection certificate required before export to Kenya; KEBS-appointed inspection agents (SGS, Intertek, Bureau Veritas); conformity with KS 1888-1 or equivalent international standard; motorcycle helmet mandatory traffic law; cycling helmet: promoted not legally mandatory
notes: Kenya: KEBS PVoC mandatory for helmet imports; KS 1888-1 = Kenyan cycling helmet standard (aligned with EN/ISO); PVoC inspection at origin country; KEBS-approved bodies conduct inspection; motorcycle helmets legally mandatory; cycling helmets: no explicit national mandate but enforcement growing

regulation_ref: SS 9:1997 (Singapore Standard — Specifications for pedal cyclists helmets)
enforcement_body: SPRING Singapore (now Enterprise Singapore) + Traffic Police
key_requirements: SS 9 is Singapore Standard for pedal cyclists helmets; voluntary compliance but de facto market requirement; Road Traffic Act (Singapore): no mandatory helmet law for cyclists on roads (mandatory for motorcyclists only); CASE (Consumers Association Singapore) may flag non-compliant products; ISO/EN 1078 equivalence accepted by market
notes: Singapore: SS 9 for cycling helmets; helmet use not legally mandatory for cyclists (mandatory for motorcyclists); SS 9 or EN 1078/CPSC compliant helmets accepted by market; Enterprise Singapore (formerly SPRING) manages standards; Consumer Protection legislation applies

regulation_name: Road Transport Act + JKR specifications — Helmet standards
regulation_ref: Road Transport Act 1987 + Malaysian Standards (MS) + SIRIM
enforcement_body: SIRIM (Standards and Industrial Research Institute of Malaysia) + JPJ (Jabatan Pengangkutan Jalan)
key_requirements: Malaysian Standards for helmets: SIRIM certification; motorcycle helmets mandatory and certified MS 1-of-1 equivalent; cycling helmets: no specific mandatory standard (Road Transport Act covers motorcycle helmets); SIRIM QAS certification for cycling helmets on voluntary basis; MS aligned with ISO/EN standards; importers: product registration SIRIM for safety products
notes: Malaysia: motorcycle helmets = mandatory SIRIM certification; cycling helmets: no national mandatory standard (recreational use); SIRIM voluntary certification accepted; EN 1078 / CPSC compliant products generally accepted for commercial sale; no mandatory cycling helmet law for cyclists

regulation_ref: TIS 282-2531 (มาตรฐานผลิตภัณฑ์อุตสาหกรรม — หมวกนิรภัยสำหรับนักปั่นจักรยาน)
enforcement_body: TISI (Thai Industrial Standards Institute) + Ministry of Industry
key_requirements: TIS 282-2531 is Thai industrial standard for bicycle helmets; TISI mandatory product inspection for some product categories (check current mandatory list); TIS certification required if on mandatory list; shock absorption; retention system; ventilation requirements; Road Traffic Act: motorcycle helmet mandatory, cycling helmet: no national mandate; importers must register products with TISI if on mandatory list
notes: Thailand: TIS 282-2531 for cycling helmets; TISI mandatory certification applies if product is on the obligatory standards list; motorcycle helmets = mandatory; cycling helmets = growing market but no national legal use mandate; TISI certification recommended for market access; Thai labelling requirements

regulation_ref: QCVN 2:2008/BKHCN (Quy chuẩn kỹ thuật quốc gia về mũ bảo hiểm) — motorcycle helmets reference for cycling
enforcement_body: BKHCN (Ministry of Science and Technology) + QUATEST
key_requirements: QCVN 2:2008/BKHCN = mandatory technical regulation for protective helmets in Vietnam; primarily covers motorcycle helmets (mandatory use) which are strongly enforced; cycling-specific helmets: may fall under same framework or treated as non-mandatory PPE; motorcycle helmets mandatory for cyclists on designated roads in some regulations; QUATEST conformity assessment; CR (conformity mark) required
notes: Vietnam: QCVN 2 primarily targets motorcycle helmets (mandatory); cycling helmets: less regulated specifically but same framework applies if classified as protective helmet; CR certification required for regulated categories; enforcement strong for motorcycle helmets, less so for cycling-specific; importers should verify current classification under BKHCN

regulation_ref: SNI 1811:2007 (Standar Nasional Indonesia — Helm pengaman untuk pemakai sepeda dan kendaraan tidak bermotor roda dua)
enforcement_body: BSN (Badan Standardisasi Nasional) + BPOM for imports
key_requirements: SNI 1811:2007 covers protective helmets for cyclists and non-motorised two-wheel vehicle users; mandatory SNI certification (Wajib SNI) if product category is listed as mandatory; SNI mark required; Lembaga Sertifikasi Produk (LSPro) accredited certification; BSN oversees standards; motorcycle helmet SNI = strictly mandatory; cycling helmet: check current mandatory SNI list; importers: BPOM and customs registration
notes: Indonesia: SNI 1811:2007 for cycling helmets; mandatory SNI certification if on Wajib SNI list; motorcycle helmets = absolutely mandatory SNI; cycling helmets: verify current mandatory status under Permenperin/SNI mandatory list; LSPro certification; Indonesian labelling (Bahasa Indonesia) required; importers register with BSN/BPOM

regulation_name: PNS 24 — Philippine National Standard for helmets
regulation_ref: PNS 24 (Philippine National Standard — Protective helmets) + Republic Act 10916 (Road Safety)
enforcement_body: DTI-BPSTD (Department of Trade and Industry — Bureau of Philippine Standards) + LTO (Land Transportation Office)
key_requirements: PNS 24 is the Philippine National Standard for protective helmets; Philippine Standard (PS) mark certification by DTI-BPS; motorcycle helmets mandatory under RA 10916; cycling helmets: LTO and traffic rules require helmet use for cyclists; DTI product certification; shock absorption; retention system; ventilation; labelling: Filipino/English; BPS certification process
notes: Philippines: PNS 24 for protective helmets including cycling; DTI-BPS certification = PS mark; RA 10916 Road Safety Act: helmet use requirements; motorcycle helmets = strictly enforced; cycling helmets: traffic rules require helmet for cyclists; BPS testing and certification; importers must register with DTI-BPS

regulation_ref: N/A — Codex scope limited to food and feed products
key_requirements: Codex standards exclusively cover food and feed products; bicycle helmets = PPE/sporting equipment = not in scope of Codex Alimentarius
notes: Codex does not regulate PPE or sporting equipment; no application to casque vélo adulte; mention for completeness of international framework review

enforcement_body: ISO TC 94/SC 6 (Personal safety — Protection of the face, eye and respiratory tract)
key_requirements: ISO 6220:1985 is the historical international reference standard for pedal cyclists' helmets; largely superseded by regional standards (EN 1078, AS/NZS 2063, CPSC 16 CFR 1203); ISO TC 94 works on PPE standards; ISO 6220 referenced by some developing-country regulatory frameworks; current ISO PPE work: TR 16236 and coordination with regional standards bodies
notes: ISO 6220 largely superseded; regional standards (EN 1078, AS/NZS 2063, CPSC) are technically superior and more widely adopted; developing countries may reference ISO 6220 in absence of national standard; ISO TC 94/SC 6 continues PPE standards work; not currently mandated anywhere as primary standard

regulation_name: UNECE WP.29 — Not Applicable (vehicle regulations only)
regulation_ref: N/A — UNECE WP.29 scope limited to vehicle technical regulations
enforcement_body: UNECE WP.29 (World Forum for Harmonization of Vehicle Regulations)
key_requirements: UNECE WP.29 develops vehicle technical regulations (automotive safety, lighting, tyres etc.); bicycle helmets are not in scope of WP.29 vehicle regulations; WP.29 does not regulate personal protective equipment or consumer sports products
notes: UNECE WP.29 = vehicle regulations framework; no application to PPE/bicycle helmets; UNECE has other working parties (WP.1 Road Safety) that promote helmet use policies but no technical product regulation; WP.1 Geneva Convention on Road Traffic promotes helmet use generally

regulation_name: WADA — Not Applicable (sports apparel/equipment not regulated)
key_requirements: WADA Prohibited List regulates substances taken by athletes (doping substances/methods); bicycle helmets as physical protective equipment are entirely outside WADA scope; helmets contain no prohibited substances; WADA does not regulate sports equipment or protective gear
notes: WADA not applicable to casque vélo adulte; helmet = passive mechanical protective equipment; no prohibited substance issue; mention for completeness of international framework review

regulation_name: OECD Guidelines — Product Safety and Due Diligence
regulation_ref: OECD Guidelines for Multinational Enterprises (2023) + OECD Recommendation on Consumer Product Safety
key_requirements: OECD Recommendation on Consumer Product Safety (2016, updated): risk assessment; coordination between national recall systems (OECD Global Recall portal); product testing transparency; supply chain traceability; Due Diligence Guidance for Responsible Business Conduct applicable to PPE manufacturing supply chains; voluntary framework
notes: OECD Recommendation on Consumer Product Safety referenced by developed country regulators; OECD Global Recall Portal integrates RAPEX (EU), CPSC recalls, ACCC recalls; PPE supply chain due diligence relevant under OECD MNE Guidelines; voluntary framework influences national regulatory approaches

regulation_name: Snell B-90A — Voluntary gold standard for bicycle helmets (US)
regulation_ref: Snell Memorial Foundation B-90A standard (voluntary certification)
key_requirements: Snell B-90A: voluntary US standard exceeding CPSC 16 CFR Part 1203 requirements; higher peak g threshold test (275g vs 300g CPSC); additional test anvils (hemi, flat, edge, rolling); more stringent retention system; widely recognized as gold standard for high-performance cycling helmets; CPSC mandatory compliance is floor; Snell certification is premium market differentiator; Snell requires annual production batch testing + factory audit
notes: Snell B-90/B-90A = voluntary but premium standard; does not replace mandatory CPSC 16 CFR 1203; used by premium helmet brands targeting US/CA/AU markets; Snell certification process: submit samples + annual production testing; Snell mark strongly valued by cycling enthusiasts and safety-conscious consumers

regulation_name: CMO Wine — Common Market Organisation Regulation
key_requirements: Framework regulation for wine market organisation; defines wine categories (PDO, PGI, varietal, generic); production practices; authorised grape varieties; minimum natural alcoholic strength; prohibition on chaptalisation in wine regions; CMO single payment scheme linkage; market measures (storage aid, crisis distillation); cross-border trade rules within EU
notes: Cornerstone of EU wine law; covers production, labelling, and market management; amended extensively by 2021/2117; applies to all 27 EU member states; wine category definitions binding across single market

key_requirements: Amends CMO (1308/2013); reinforces PDO (Protected Designation of Origin) and PGI (Protected Geographical Indication) rules for wine; updated product specifications requirements; enhanced controls on GI protection; new provisions for wine tourism and communication; sustainability requirements; vineyard register rules; optional quality terms regulation
notes: CRITICAL reform effective 2023-2024; all existing wine GIs must comply; producers updating product specifications must follow new procedure; Champagne, Bordeaux, Rioja, Chianti, Barolo all affected; EUIPO manages GI database eAmbrosia

enforcement_body: EC DG AGRI + national competent authorities
key_requirements: Detailed labelling rules for PDO and PGI wines; mandatory particulars: category of grapevine product; actual alcoholic strength; lot number; nominal volume; allergen statements (sulfites mandatory); importer details; optional particulars rules; bottle shape restrictions for certain PDOs; e-label possibility introduced in 2021/2117 amendment; font size requirements
notes: 2019/33 = presentation rules; 2019/34 = technical specifications for GI protection procedures; sulfite declaration mandatory if >10 mg/L SO2; allergen labelling critical; e-label for ingredient/nutrition info since Dec 2023

regulation_name: Food Information to Consumers — Ingredients + Nutrition (Wine)
enforcement_body: EC DG SANTE + national food authorities
key_requirements: From 8 December 2023: wine must display list of ingredients and nutrition declaration; can use e-label (QR code) for full list but energy mandatory on physical label; ingredients include grape, sulfur dioxide, egg/milk-based fining agents (allergens); nutrition per 100 ml (energy, fat, carbs, sugars, protein, salt); voluntary front-of-pack nutrition labelling; Nutri-Score optional but must comply if used
notes: MAJOR new obligation from Dec 2023 - many producers not yet compliant; ingredient declaration on QR code permitted; energy value must appear on bottle; fining agents (egg white, isinglass, casein) = allergen declaration mandatory; wine category = FIC Annex VII exemption ended Dec 2023

regulation_ref: Directive 2011/64/EU (Council Directive on excise alcohol structure)
enforcement_body: Council of the EU + national tax authorities
key_requirements: Harmonises structure of excise duty on alcoholic beverages in EU; defines wine category (still and sparkling); minimum excise rate for wine = 0 EUR/hl (member states free to set own rates); wine taxed per hectolitre; reduced rates for small producers; EMCS movement control; duty suspension regime for intra-EU trade; warehousekeepers and registered consignees regime; excise duty point at release for consumption
notes: EU minimum excise on wine = 0 EUR/hl but member states apply own rates; France approx 3.75 EUR/hl still wine; DE approx 0 (historic exemption); IT varies; excise must be paid at destination for cross-border sales; EMCS electronic tracking mandatory for duty-suspended movements

regulation_name: EMCS — Excise Movement and Control System
enforcement_body: EC DG TAXUD + national customs/excise authorities
key_requirements: Electronic system for tracking duty-suspended movements of excise goods (including wine) within EU; mandatory e-Administrative Document (e-AD) for all intra-EU wine movements under duty suspension; Accompanying Document required for duty-paid movements; ARC (Administrative Reference Code) on each movement; real-time monitoring; export procedures; loss/shortage reporting
notes: EMCS mandatory for all EU intra-community wine trade in duty suspension; wineries must be registered as warehousekeepers; importers need SEED registration; ARC number required before physical movement; EMCS Phase 3 fully deployed

regulation_name: Public Health (Alcohol) Act 2018 Ireland — avertissement sante obligatoire mai 2026
regulation_ref: Public Health (Alcohol) Act 2018 (Ireland) + SI No. 406 of 2023 (Health labelling commencement)
enforcement_body: Health Service Executive (HSE) + Revenue Commissioners + Department of Health Ireland
key_requirements: Ireland Public Health (Alcohol) Act 2018: MANDATORY health warnings on wine labels sold in Ireland from 22 May 2026; warning must include: (1) health warning text about cancer link; (2) calorie information per serving and per 100ml; (3) grams of alcohol; (4) HSE.ie website reference; (5) pregnancy pictogram (woman and wine glass crossed); label space requirements: minimum 1/8 of label back; cannot be obscured; applies to all wine sold in Ireland regardless of origin; importers and retailers responsible for compliance; FIRST EU member state to mandate cancer-specific alcohol warnings
notes: CRITICAL - Ireland = first EU country to mandate alcohol-cancer link warning on bottles (May 2026); all wine exported to Ireland must have compliant label by 22 May 2026; EU Commission initially challenged under SGEI; WTO notification concerns from US, Australia wine producers; compliance requires label redesign for Irish market; may set EU precedent for pan-EU labelling reform

regulation_ref: Code rural et de la peche maritime, Articles L. 641-1 to L. 641-28 + decrets AOC
enforcement_body: INAO (Institut National de l'Origine et de la Qualite) + DGCCRF
key_requirements: INAO manages all French AOC/AOP wine designations (Bordeaux, Bourgogne, Champagne, Cotes du Rhone etc.); product specifications (cahiers des charges): grape varieties, yields, alcohol levels, ageing requirements, vineyard demarcation; annual harvest declaration (declaration de recolte); yield authorisation (rendements); INAO certification mandatory before AOC labelling; operators register with ODG (organismes de defense et de gestion)
notes: France = largest AOP wine producer globally; 360+ AOC/AOP designations; INAO audits mandatory; non-compliance = loss of AOC status; Champagne designation particularly strictly protected; yields and chaptalization rules vary by appellation

regulation_ref: Loi n 91-32 du 10 janvier 1991 (Loi Evin) + Decret d'application
key_requirements: Severely restricts alcohol advertising in France; permitted media: print press (adult-targeted), radio (specific hours), internet (static information sites only), wine/drinks specialist events, branded product/packaging; PROHIBITED: TV advertising of alcohol; ban on sponsorship of events broadcast on TV; advertising must include government health message; internet advertising for alcohol companies via social media heavily restricted
notes: Loi Evin = strictest alcohol advertising law in EU; conflicts with digital marketing strategies; social media campaigns for wine brands require legal clearance; health warning mandatory on all ads: L'abus d'alcool est dangereux pour la sante

regulation_ref: Code des douanes art. 401 et suivants + CGI + decrets CRD
enforcement_body: DGDDI (Direction Generale des Douanes et Droits Indirects)
key_requirements: French excise duty on wine: still wine approx 3.75 EUR/hl; sparkling (Champagne) approx 9.46 EUR/hl; capsule representative de droit (CRD) mandatory on bottles sold in France; CRD = tax capsule on cork/closure indicating excise paid; different CRD colours for different alcohol categories; recoltant-manipulant vs negociant-manipulant rules; entrepots fiscaux agrees (EFA) for bonded warehouses; tax return monthly/quarterly
notes: CRD = physical evidence of excise payment on French wine; mandatory for all wine sold retail in France; Champagne CRD must show CM (cooperative de manipulation) or RM (recoltant-manipulant) or NM (negociant-manipulant) status

regulation_ref: Weingesetz vom 16. Mai 2001 (BGBl. I S. 985) + Weinverordnung (WeinV)
enforcement_body: Bundesministerium fuer Ernaehrung und Landwirtschaft (BMEL) + Landesweinbehoerden
key_requirements: Federal wine law implementing EU CMO requirements; defines quality categories: Qualitatswein bestimmter Anbaugebiete (QbA), Pradikatswein (Kabinett through Trockenbeerenauslese); 13 defined wine regions (Anbaugebiete): Mosel, Rheingau, Pfalz, Baden etc.; Amtliche Pruefungsnummer (AP-Nr) mandatory for quality wines - official taste testing; permitted grape varieties per region; chaptalisation rules; must weight (Ochsle) minimum requirements; Weinbaukartei (vineyard cadastre)
notes: German Pradikat system (Spatlese, Auslese, Beerenauslese, TBA) = world-renowned quality tier; AP number mandatory on label for QbA+; each wine lot must pass official panel tasting; VDP (Verband Deutscher Pradikatsweingueter) additional classification (Grosse Lage etc.) voluntary but market important

key_requirements: Germany historically had zero excise duty on wine — unique EU position; wine exempt from German excise tax (beer and spirits taxed); EU directive wine minimum is 0 EUR/hl so compliant; EU import wine subject to customs duty + VAT; spirits taxed under AlkStG; wine-specific: no excise but VAT at 19%; EU EMCS applies for intra-EU movements; Import Declaration required for third-country wine
notes: Germany = only major EU wine market with zero domestic excise on wine; tax competitive advantage for wine vs beer/spirits; planned EU alcohol duty harmonisation discussions ongoing; German wine sector strongly opposes any wine excise introduction

regulation_name: Testo Unico della Vite e del Vino
regulation_ref: Decreto Legislativo 8 aprile 2010, n. 61 + D.Lgs 238/2016 (Testo Unico del Vino)
enforcement_body: MIPAAF (Ministero delle Politiche Agricole) + ICQRF + Consorzi di tutela
key_requirements: Comprehensive Italian wine law; defines DOC (Denominazione di Origine Controllata), DOCG (Controllata e Garantita), IGT (Indicazione Geografica Tipica); DOCG wines must pass DOCG panel tasting + government bottleneck seal (fascetta); production specifications (disciplinari) per denomination; National vine register; vineyard declaration; harvest declaration; bottling restrictions (some DOCG must be bottled in zone); consorzi enforcement
notes: Italy = world's largest wine producer; 77 DOCG + 341 DOC + approx 120 IGT; DOCG fascetta (neck label) mandatory government seal; Barolo, Brunello, Amarone, Prosecco DOC = major global exports; ICQRF anti-fraud controls; fascetta costs and allocation system

regulation_ref: D.M. disciplinari individuali per chaque DOC/DOCG + circulaires MIPAAF
key_requirements: Each Italian DOC/DOCG has individual disciplinare (product specification); Prosecco DOC covers northeast Italy; Chianti Classico DOCG gallo nero seal; Barolo/Barbaresco DOCG yield max 8t/ha; Amarone della Valpolicella DOCG appassimento rules; Franciacorta DOCG sparkling; denominazione di origini protetta (DOP) = EU alignment; export certificates for third countries via SIAN electronic system
notes: Prosecco DOC = highest volume Italian GI wine; Prosecco DOCG (Conegliano-Valdobbiadene) = premium; Italian DOC fraud (sounding names) actively prosecuted; ICQRF random sampling at winery, wholesale, retail levels

regulation_name: Ley de la Vina y del Vino
regulation_ref: Ley 24/2003, de 10 de julio, de la Vina y del Vino (+ Real Decretos de desarrollo)
enforcement_body: MAPA (Ministerio de Agricultura) + CCAA (Comunidades Autonomas) + AEAT
key_requirements: Spanish wine framework law; defines DOP (Denominacion de Origen Protegida), IGP (Indicacion Geografica Protegida), Vino de Pago; 69 DO + 2 DOCa (Rioja, Priorat); regulatory councils (Consejos Reguladores) manage each DO; label approval mandatory; analytical and organoleptic certification; harvest register; production limits; crianza/reserva/gran reserva ageing requirements; mandatory health warning on label
notes: Spain = 3rd largest wine producer; Rioja DOCa + Priorat DOCa = highest tier; CCAA share competency with state; Cava (sparkling) DO specific rules; mandatory health warning El alcohol perjudica su salud on Spanish market; export volumes significant to US, Germany, UK

regulation_name: DOP/DO/IGP system + Impuesto sobre el alcohol (excise Espagne)
enforcement_body: AEAT (Agencia Estatal de Administracion Tributaria) + CAEs
key_requirements: Spain: wine (still, less than 15% ABV) = zero excise duty; sparkling wine = excise applies; Impuesto Especial sobre el Alcohol applies to distilled spirits; wine exempt following EU structure directive; VAT 21% standard; registered tax warehouse (almacen fiscal) required; intra-EU EMCS compliance; export refund procedure via customs electronic system; DO-level tracking via cuaderno de bodega (winery logbook)
notes: Spain follows EU zero minimum excise on still wine; sparkling wine subject to Spanish excise at 0 EUR historically also 0; spirits heavily taxed; wine vs cava fiscal treatment creates market distortion discussions

regulation_name: Wet op de Accijns + Drank- en Horecawet (Pays-Bas)
regulation_ref: Wet op de Accijns (Accijnswet) + Drank- en Horecawet (DHW) 2013
enforcement_body: Belastingdienst (Tax Authority) + NVWA (Netherlands Food and Consumer Safety Authority)
key_requirements: Netherlands excise on wine: still wine 0.00 EUR/hl (EU minimum); sparkling wine approx 66.76 EUR/hl; licence required for retail and on-trade sale of alcohol (DHW licence); age verification 18+ mandatory; NVWA controls food safety and labelling compliance; supermarket wine sales permitted; online sales age verification required; no monopoly system; EMCS compliance for EU trade; import via Rotterdam major entry point
notes: Netherlands = major EU wine import/distribution hub (Rotterdam port); Dutch distribution companies handle significant portion of EU wine trade; DHW licence required for retail/hospitality; minimum age 18 strictly enforced since 2014

regulation_ref: The Wine Regulations 2011 (SI 2011/2936) as retained in UK law post-Brexit + UK Alcohol Wholesaler Registration Scheme
enforcement_body: HMRC + Food Standards Agency (FSA) + Trading Standards
key_requirements: UK retained Wine Regulations 2011 post-Brexit; defines wine categories, quality designations; UK GI scheme (UK PDO/PGI) replacing EU GI for domestic; mandatory labelling: product name, volume, ABV, lot number, allergens (sulfites), country of origin, importer; English wine industry growing (English sparkling wine); AWRS mandatory for wholesale alcohol businesses; anti-money laundering controls; Trading Standards enforcement
notes: Post-Brexit: UK no longer recognises EU GI automatically; mutual recognition agreement under TCA; UK PDO/PGI registration separate process; EU wine exports to UK subject to UK import controls; Sanitary/Phytosanitary checks; new UK wine style designations under development

regulation_name: Alcohol Duty Act 2023 — New UK duty regime
regulation_ref: Finance (No.2) Act 2023 — Alcohol Duty provisions (effective 1 August 2023)
key_requirements: RADICAL reform of UK alcohol duty from August 2023; duty now based on ABV strength (per percentage point) across all categories; wine duty rates: still wine 11.5% ABV = approx GBP 2.67/bottle (75cl); rates graduated by strength; sparkling wine now taxed differently (previously premium rate eliminated); wine-in-range (11.5%-14.5% ABV) flat rate transitional relief until 2025; draught relief for on-trade; new duty stamps for spirits above threshold; duty calculator mandatory for pricing
notes: MAJOR CHANGE August 2023 - all wine importers/producers must recalculate duty on ABV basis; wine 11.5% ABV = lower duty than 14.5%; incentive for lower-ABV wines; transitional wine-in-range relief originally to Feb 2025, extended; Scotch whisky industry opposed reform; wine industry compliance costs significant

regulation_name: Minimum Unit Pricing Scotland 50p/65p per unit
regulation_ref: Alcohol (Minimum Pricing) (Scotland) Act 2012 + SSI 2018/100 + SSI 2024 amendment
enforcement_body: Scottish Government + Public Health Scotland + Trading Standards Scotland
key_requirements: Scotland MUP: minimum 50p per unit (10ml pure alcohol); bottle of wine (75cl, 13% ABV) = minimum GBP 4.87; reviewed 2023 - MUP increased to 65p/unit from September 2024; applies to all alcohol retail sales in Scotland; on-trade exempt from MUP (licence pricing different); off-trade supermarket/off-licence must comply; imports to Scotland must comply when sold; Wales introduced MUP 2020 (50p/unit); England no MUP
notes: Scotland MUP 65p/unit from Sept 2024 = effective wine floor price approx GBP 6.34/bottle (13% ABV 75cl); significant impact on discount/bulk wine market; Wales MUP 50p/unit; England no MUP - regulatory divergence within UK; health evidence review positive (reduced consumption, alcohol-related deaths)

regulation_ref: Loi federale sur l'alcool du 21 juin 1932 (LAlc, RS 680) + OAlc (RS 680.11)
key_requirements: Switzerland: wine fermented from grapes under 18% ABV not subject to federal alcohol monopoly; wine regulated under food law (ODAl); labelling must comply with Swiss law (parallel to EU FIC); alcoholic strength declaration mandatory; sulfite declaration mandatory; Swiss quality wine categories (cantonal AOC); import duty on wine (customs tariff); Swiss-EU bilateral agri agreement on wine; no specific wine excise for still wine under 18% ABV
notes: Switzerland maintains its own wine regime separate from EU (not EEA member); Swiss bilateral agreements with EU on agriculture include wine; Swiss wines: Valais AOC, Vaud AOC, Geneve AOC, Neuchatel AOC; imported wine must meet Swiss labelling requirements (German/French/Italian); Swissmedic not involved (food authority OSAV/OFSP)

regulation_name: AOC cantonaux + protections IGP Suisse viticoles
regulation_ref: Ordonnance sur la viticulture et l'importation du vin (RS 916.140) + reglements cantonaux AOC
key_requirements: Swiss cantonal AOC system: each wine-producing canton has own AOC regulations; Valais (Fendant, Dole), Vaud (Chasselas), Geneve, Neuchatel, Zurich etc.; cantonal rules define grape varieties, yields, alcoholic strength; Swiss Federal Office of Agriculture oversees import tariffs; wine imports from EU under bilateral agreement; third-country wine import tariffs apply; Swiss AOC not automatically recognised in EU (bilateral negotiations)
notes: Swiss cantonal AOC complexity requires vineyard-by-vineyard declaration; Chasselas (Gutedel) = iconic Swiss white grape; Switzerland largest per-capita wine consumer in world; imports significant from France, Italy, Spain

regulation_ref: Lov om omsetning av alkoholholdig drikk m.v. (Alkoholloven) LOV-1989-06-02-27
key_requirements: Norway: strict state monopoly on retail sale of wine and spirits via Vinmonopolet (government-owned); private companies cannot retail wine to consumers; grocery stores limited to beer max 4.7% ABV; restaurants/hotels licence required; Vinmonopolet = only legal off-trade retail point for wine; limited opening hours; wine listing process via Vinmonopolet tender system; import requires Norwegian Food Authority approval; labelling: Norwegian language requirements
notes: Vinmonopolet = compulsory sales channel for all wine retail in Norway; wine producers/importers must apply for Vinmonopolet listing; competitive tender process; limited hours (Mon-Fri until 18h, Sat until 15h); significant barrier to market entry; Norway not EU member but EEA - some EMCS obligations via bilateral agreement; wine excise approx NOK 4.78/12% ABV 75cl bottle

regulation_name: Loi federale 171-FZ sur la reglementation de l'alcool
regulation_ref: Federal Law No. 171-FZ of 22 November 1995 (as amended to 2024)
enforcement_body: Rosalkogolregulirovanie (RAR) - Federal Alcohol Market Regulatory Service
key_requirements: Russia: federal licensing for wine production, wholesale, retail; EGAIS (Unified State Automated Information System) mandatory for all alcohol movement tracking - electronic marking; excise stamps (Federal Special Mark) mandatory on all wine bottles; excise rates on wine: still wine approx 21 RUB/litre; sparkling approx 45 RUB/litre; mandatory labelling in Russian; sulfite declaration; country of origin; grape variety; prohibited sales to minors under 18; restricted hours (22:00-10:00 in most regions)
notes: EGAIS = mandatory electronic tracking for all Russian wine trade; each bottle gets excise stamp; import wine requires RAR importer registration; counterfeit wine major issue historically; sanctions since 2022 complicate EU wine exports to Russia; domestic Russian wine production growing (Krasnodar region)

regulation_name: TR TS 047/2018 — Reglement technique EAEU boissons alcoolisees
regulation_ref: Technical Regulation of the Customs Union TR TS 047/2018 (alcoholic products)
key_requirements: EAEU technical regulation harmonising alcoholic beverages rules across Russia, Belarus, Kazakhstan, Armenia, Kyrgyzstan; wine definitions, quality standards; labelling requirements: product name, ABV, volume, manufacturer, country of origin, composition, contraindications for pregnant women/drivers; health warning statements mandatory; microbiological and physicochemical safety standards; storage temperature requirements; prohibited additives list
notes: TR TS 047/2018 mandatory for all alcohol sold in EAEU markets; wine importers must declare conformity; labelling health warning mandatory: alcohol contraindicated during pregnancy, for minors, when driving; each EAEU country may have additional national requirements (excise, licensing)

regulation_name: TAPDK / Tarim Bakanligi — reglementation alcool Turquie
regulation_ref: Kanun No. 4733 (Tutun ve Alkol Piyasasi Duzenleme Kurumu) + Gida Yonetmelikleri
enforcement_body: TAPDK (Tutun ve Alkol Piyasasi Duzenleme Kurumu) - now integrated into Ministry of Agriculture
key_requirements: Turkey: alcohol production and sale regulated; licence required for manufacturing, importing, wholesaling retail; significant excise: OTV (Ozel Tuketim Vergisi) on wine - rate approximately 63.3 TRY/litre + ad valorem component; total tax burden on wine very high (over 50% of retail price); advertising of alcohol prohibited on TV, radio; advertising restricted to adult platforms only; health warning mandatory on label; no sale to under-18s; restricted sale hours; wine sold in licensed venues and specific retailers
notes: Turkey has significant Muslim population - political pressure on alcohol availability; excise rates increased sharply in recent years; import duties + OTV make imported wine expensive; domestic wine production in Anatolia (Kavaklidere, Doluca brands); Ramadan sales restrictions in some municipalities

regulation_name: Loi sur la reglementation de la production et du commerce d'alcool Ukraine
regulation_ref: Zakon Ukrainy Pro derzhavne rehulyuvannya vyrobnytstva i obihu spirtu No. 481/95-VR + amendments
enforcement_body: Derzhprodspozyvsluzh (State Service for Food Safety and Consumer Protection) + DFS (Customs)
key_requirements: Ukraine: licence mandatory for wine production, import, wholesale; excise stamps mandatory on wine bottles; excise rate on wine: approx UAH 8.42/litre still wine; labelling in Ukrainian mandatory; country of origin, grape variety, ABV, manufacturer; Ukrainian wine GI system developing (Odessa, Transcarpathia regions); minimum retail price controls; restricted sale hours (22:00-10:00); prohibited sale to under-18s; FSCP food safety controls
notes: Ukraine wine production significant before 2022 war disruption (Crimea annexation 2014 = loss of major wine region); war since 2022 severely disrupts wine industry; import wine market challenged by exchange rate; Ukraine GI system EU-alignment under AA/DCFTA

regulation_name: Federal Alcohol Administration Act + TTB Wine Labelling
regulation_ref: 27 USC ss 201-212 (FAA Act) + 27 CFR Part 4 (Wine Labelling and Advertising)
enforcement_body: TTB (Alcohol and Tobacco Tax and Trade Bureau) - Treasury Dept
key_requirements: Federal TTB approval required: Certificate of Label Approval (COLA) mandatory for all wine sold in interstate commerce; mandatory label information: brand name, class/type, appellation of origin, alcohol content, net contents, name and address of bottler/importer, sulfite declaration (if 10 ppm or more), Surgeon General's health warning; vintage date requirements; varietal (75% minimum); American Viticultural Area (AVA) designation (85% grapes from AVA); TTB formula approval for some wine products
notes: COLA = mandatory TTB pre-market label approval; process takes 30-90 days; importer must have TTB Basic Permit; 270+ AVAs defined; wine definition strict (fermented grapes); Surgeon General's warning two-part mandatory statement on all US wine labels; specific bottle sizes regulated

regulation_name: Surgeon General's Mandatory Health Warning on Alcohol
regulation_ref: Public Health Service Act s215 + 27 CFR Part 16 (mandatory alcohol warning)
key_requirements: Mandatory warning statement on all US wine labels since 1988 (ALAA): (1) According to the Surgeon General, women should not drink alcoholic beverages during pregnancy because of the risk of birth defects. (2) Consumption of alcoholic beverages impairs your ability to drive a car or operate machinery, and may cause health problems. Warning must be in minimum type size; conspicuous placement; 2024: Surgeon General's Advisory calling for updated cancer risk warnings - regulatory process underway
notes: 2024 Surgeon General Advisory recommends updating alcohol warning to include cancer risk (7 types of cancer linked to alcohol); regulatory change proposal underway - watch for FDA/TTB rulemaking; current 1988 warning still legally required; bipartite warning format mandatory

regulation_name: Federal wine excise tax + 21st Amendment state jurisdiction
regulation_ref: 27 CFR Part 24 (Wine Production and Tax) + 26 USC s5041 (IRC excise) + 21st Amendment
key_requirements: Federal excise tax on wine: still wine 14% ABV or less = USD 1.07/gallon; sparkling = USD 3.40/gallon; hard cider = USD 0.226/gallon; small producer credit up to 250,000 gallons; 21st Amendment gives states broad authority over alcohol; 3-tier system mandatory in most states (producer-distributor-retailer); direct-to-consumer shipping varies by state; dry counties still exist in some states (especially Bible Belt - Kentucky, Tennessee, Mississippi, Texas rural areas); open container laws; blue laws (Sunday restrictions)
notes: 21st Amendment creates massive state-by-state variation; some states (PA, UT, NH) maintain state liquor stores; 3-tier system prevents producers from bypassing distributors; direct-to-consumer wine shipping legal in approx 45 states with various permit requirements; dry county maps essential for distribution planning

regulation_name: California ABC — Alcoholic Beverage Control licensing
regulation_ref: California Business and Professions Code ss 23000-25762 + 4 CCR (ABC regulations)
enforcement_body: California Department of Alcoholic Beverage Control (ABC)
key_requirements: California ABC licence required for all wine retail, on-trade, manufacturing, importing; Type 02 (winery), Type 20 (off-licence beer/wine), Type 21 (off-licence all alcohol), Type 47 (on-sale general) licences; licence transfer restrictions; tied-house restrictions (producer cannot own retail in California generally); direct-to-consumer shipping: California winery to CA consumer = permitted; out-of-state winery DTC requires CA ABC licence; winery licence allows limited special events
notes: California = largest US wine market and production region (Napa, Sonoma, Central Valley); CA ABC issues approx 90,000+ active licences; CA winery can ship DTC to consumers in approx 45 states; tied-house restrictions complex; CA requires licensed carrier for DTC shipments

regulation_name: Proposition 65 — Lead + alcohol carcinogen warning wine Californie
regulation_ref: California Safe Drinking Water and Toxic Enforcement Act (Prop 65) + Cal/EPA OEHHA
enforcement_body: OEHHA + California Attorney General + private enforcers
key_requirements: Prop 65: alcohol (ethanol) is listed carcinogen and reproductive toxicant; wine must carry Prop 65 warning in California unless below safe harbour level (alcohol in wine is above); required warning for wine sold in CA: CONTAINS CHEMICALS including alcohol known to State of California to cause cancer and birth defects or other reproductive harm; lead also historically found in some wine capsules (now largely phased out); point-of-sale signs, menu warnings, or bottle labels required
notes: Prop 65 warning mandatory on ALL wine sold in California (alcohol = listed carcinogen); warning must be given before point of sale for on-trade; failure = private enforcement lawsuit (bounty hunter litigation extremely active); safe harbour for alcohol not established (exceeds threshold); major compliance issue for all wine sold in CA

regulation_name: NY State Liquor Authority (SLA) — wine licensing New York
regulation_ref: Alcoholic Beverage Control Law (NY ABC Law) + 9 NYCRR (SLA Regulations)
key_requirements: NY SLA regulates all alcoholic beverage commerce in New York State; winery licence (Farm Winery - NY Agriculture and Markets Law Art. 6, s76-a): allows winery tasting rooms, retail on-site, DTC shipping within NY; off-premises retail licence required for wine stores; on-premises licence for restaurants; 3-tier system applies; DTC shipping from out-of-state wineries permitted since 2005 (Granholm v. Heald Supreme Court); grocery stores permitted to sell wine in NY since specific legislation
notes: NY Farm Winery licence encourages NY grape growing (Finger Lakes, Long Island wine regions); NYC = major wine consumption market; SLA enforcement of advertising restrictions; NY DTC shipping requires NY SLA registration for out-of-state wineries; unlicensed wine shipments = significant penalties

regulation_name: Excise Act 2001 + Food and Drug Regulations (vin Canada federal)
regulation_ref: Excise Act, 2001 (S.C. 2002, c. 22) + Food and Drug Regulations (FDR) Division 2
enforcement_body: CRA (Canada Revenue Agency) + provincial liquor boards + CFIA
key_requirements: Canada: federal excise duty on wine = CAD 0.688/litre (still wine); sparkling = CAD 0.704/litre; wine licence required from CRA; provincial import and distribution via liquor boards: LCBO (Ontario), SAQ (Quebec), BCLDB (BC), AGLC (Alberta), NSLC etc.; most provinces = monopoly import/distribution model; private wine store growth in some provinces; labelling: bilingual (English/French) mandatory per Food and Drug Regulations; grade labelling (Vintners Quality Alliance - VQA for Canadian wine)
notes: Canada provincial liquor boards = compulsory import agent for most wine; LCBO/SAQ/BCLDB listing required; private wine store growth but still board-dominant; interprovincial wine trade liberalised (Bill C-311 2012, various provincial reforms); VQA certification for Canadian wines; Indigenous-owned winery exemptions in some provinces

regulation_name: Reglementation provinciale alcool + appellations canadiennes VQA
regulation_ref: Provincial Liquor Acts (Ontario Liquor Licence and Control Act; BC Liquor Control and Licensing Act etc.) + VQA Ontario Act + BC Wine Authority
enforcement_body: Provincial liquor authorities + VQA Ontario + BC Wine Authority
key_requirements: Provincial control: LCBO (Ontario) = Crown corporation controlling import and most retail; SAQ (Quebec) = similar model; Alberta = privatised retail model; BC = hybrid (BC Liquor Stores + private); VQA (Vintners Quality Alliance) certification for Canadian wines: 100% Canadian grapes, regulated appellations (Niagara Peninsula, Okanagan Valley etc.); Canadian wine import from US, EU subject to CETA tariff preferences (EU wine); USMCA wine tariff schedules
notes: Canadian provincial alcohol regulations = patchwork; interprovincial wine trade still restricted in some respects; Okanagan Valley (BC) and Niagara Peninsula (ON) = major Canadian wine regions; CETA reduces EU wine tariffs in Canada to zero (phased); Canadian wine GI protection developing

enforcement_body: COFEPRIS (Comision Federal para la Proteccion contra Riesgos Sanitarios) + SE (Secretaria de Economia)
key_requirements: Mandatory labelling for all alcoholic beverages sold in Mexico: product name, alcohol content (% vol), net content, country of origin, importer details, lot number; health warning mandatory; prohibicion de venta a menores warning; COFEPRIS sanitary registration mandatory for imported wine; labelling in Spanish; geographic indications for wine (Baja California wine region growing)
notes: Mexico wine market growing - Baja California (Valle de Guadalupe) = main production region; wine imports from Spain, Chile, Argentina, France, US dominant; COFEPRIS registration mandatory for all imported wine; Denominations of Origin developing; NOM compliance certification required

regulation_name: IEPS — Impuesto Especial sobre Produccion y Servicios (alcool Mexique)
regulation_ref: Ley del Impuesto Especial sobre Produccion y Servicios (LIEPS) + SAT administration
key_requirements: Mexico IEPS excise on wine: ad valorem rate 26.5% on price of wine; applies at manufacturer/importer level; import wine: customs duty + IEPS + IVA (16% VAT); total tax burden significant; marbete (tax seal) mandatory on wine bottles - electronic marbete system; annual marbete quota for importers; IEPS paid on importation via pedimento (customs declaration); wine sold without marbete = illegal; electronic invoicing (CFDI) mandatory for all wine sales
notes: Marbete = physical/electronic tax stamp on each wine bottle in Mexico; importers must register with SAT and obtain marbete quota; marbete applied at bonded warehouse before release to market; customs value (valor en aduana) + IEPS + IVA = landed cost basis; significant grey market challenge

regulation_ref: Lei 7.678/1988 (producao vinho) + MAPA Decreto 8.198/2014 + ANVISA RDC 14/2014
enforcement_body: MAPA (Ministerio da Agricultura) + ANVISA (Agencia Nacional de Vigilancia Sanitaria) + IBRAVIN
key_requirements: Brazil: wine production, import, and sale regulated; MAPA oversees viticulture and wine authentication; ANVISA = food safety/labelling; wine labelling mandatory in Portuguese: product name, producer, origin, grape variety, vintage, alcohol content, volume, lot; importers require MAPA registration; phytosanitary certificate for imported wine; Vale dos Vinhedos (RS) = first Brazilian appellation with EU GI recognition; wine excise: IPI on wine; IBRAVIN = Brazilian Wine Institute promotes exports
notes: Brazil = significant wine producer (Rio Grande do Sul - Bento Goncalves region); domestic market dominated by Argentinian/Chilean imports; Vale dos Vinhedos = EU-recognised Brazilian GI; ANVISA labelling controls; MAPA anti-fraud controls; wine imports growing with rising middle class

key_requirements: Brazil import wine taxes: Import Duty (II) = 27% on CIF value; IPI (Imposto sobre Produtos Industrializados) = 0% for wines (classified NCM 2204); PIS/COFINS import = 2.1% + 9.65% = 11.75%; ICMS (state VAT) = 12-25% depending on state; total effective import tax burden = approx 50-65% of CIF value; wine classified under NCM/HS 2204; MAPA registration + phytosanitary clearance required; Brazilian Real exchange rate risk
notes: High import tax burden makes Brazil one of most expensive wine import markets globally; domestic wine (RS region) has significant tax advantages; Argentina/Chile MERCOSUR zero-tariff wine = major competitive advantage vs EU/US wine; Receita Federal controls import declaration (DI)

regulation_ref: Ley Nacional de Vinos 14.878 (1959 as amended) + Instituto Nacional de Vitivinicultura (INV)
key_requirements: Argentina's foundational wine law; INV = national wine regulatory institute based in Mendoza; mandatory registration of vineyards, wineries, wine products; analytical certification for each wine lot before sale; INV tasting committee for denominaciones de origen (Lujan de Cuyo DOC, Maipu DOC, San Rafael DO etc.); Malbec GI protection developing; wine classification system (comun, finos, reserva, gran reserva); label approval by INV; phytosanitary certificates for export; mandatory lot number and production batch tracking
notes: Argentina = 5th largest wine producer; Mendoza = 70% of production; Malbec = flagship variety; INV certification required for every wine batch; export certificate mandatory; Lujan de Cuyo = first Argentine DOC; Vino Argentino export program; US, EU, Brazil = main export markets

key_requirements: Argentina: Impuestos Internos on wine = 0% (wine exempt from internal excise taxes); wine subject to standard VAT (IVA) 21%; import wine subject to import duty (arancel externo comun Mercosur) + statistics rate + VAT; INCOTERM CIF + 12% import duty for non-Mercosur wine; Mercosur members (Brazil, Chile assoc.) benefit from zero tariff; AFIP controls tax compliance; withholding taxes on remittances; official exchange rate complex
notes: Argentina wine excise = 0% (historical policy to protect wine culture); economic instability (inflation) impacts wine pricing; export reimbursements (reintegros de exportacion) historically available; Mercosur tariff = 0% for intra-zone wine trade; AFIP increasingly digitalised

regulation_ref: Ley N 18.455 (Dictates rules on production, elaboration and marketing of alcoholic beverages) + SAG (Servicio Agricola y Ganadero) Wine Regulation
enforcement_body: SAG (Servicio Agricola y Ganadero) + SII (Servicio de Impuestos Internos) + SEREMI Salud
key_requirements: Chile: comprehensive wine law; SAG regulates wine production, labels, export certificates; mandatory SAG registration of wineries and vineyards; wine categories: Vino Corriente, Vino con Denominacion de Origen; DOs: Valle del Maipo, Valle de Colchagua, Valle de Casablanca etc.; label approval by SAG; varietal composition declaration; export health certificate; Chilean wine must meet technical specs; age verification 18+ for purchase; health warning mandatory on label
notes: Chile = world's 4th largest wine exporter; SAG wine export certificates mandatory; Chilean DOs not yet legally protected in all markets; EU-Chile FTA includes wine GI mutual recognition; Carmenenere = Chilean flagship variety

regulation_ref: Decreto 78/1986 Ministerio de Agricultura (Zones demarcation) + Ley 18.455
key_requirements: Chilean wine DOs: legally defined producing zones; Valle Central, Aconcagua, Coquimbo, Austral, Atacama major zones + sub-zones; vintage year mandatory if specified variety/origin; Chilean excise on wine: ILA (Impuesto de Lujo Adicional) - wine historically exempt; standard VAT (IVA) 19%; import wine: customs duty (general rate, free trade agreement preferential rates); EU wine enters at 0% under EU-Chile FTA; Carnet de vitivinicultor mandatory for producers
notes: Chile FTAs cover 90% of wine export destinations (US, EU, China, Japan, Canada all have FTAs); tariff advantages significant; Chilean DO system less strict than EU GI (producers can blend zones); Concha y Toro, Santa Carolina = major exporters

regulation_ref: Decreto 1686 de 2012 (por el cual se establece el reglamento tecnico sobre los requisitos sanitarios de bebidas alcoholicas) + INVIMA resoluciones
enforcement_body: INVIMA (Instituto Nacional de Vigilancia de Medicamentos y Alimentos) + DIAN
key_requirements: Colombia: INVIMA sanitary registration (Registro Sanitario) mandatory for all wine products sold in Colombia; technical regulation: physico-chemical specifications (ABV min 7%, max 14% for wine); labelling in Spanish: product name, alcohol content, volume, manufacturer, importer, lot, expiry if applicable; sulfite warning if applicable; import permit required; phytosanitary clearance; customs import declaration via MUISCA system; wine market growing in urban centres
notes: Colombia wine market small but growing (Bogota, Medellin upper-middle class); INVIMA RS mandatory = 3-6 month process; Chilean, Spanish, Argentine wine dominate; Andean Pact (CAN) wine rules; Colombian wine production minimal; DIAN = customs + tax authority

regulation_name: Ley 1816/2016 — monopolio estatal licores + taxes departementales vin
regulation_ref: Ley 1816 de 2016 (sobre el monopolio rentistico de licores destilados) + decreto reglamentario
enforcement_body: Gobernaciones departamentales + Superintendencia de Industria y Comercio
key_requirements: Colombia: departmental monopoly applies primarily to distilled spirits (aguardiente, rum); wine is NOT subject to the spirits monopoly under Law 1816; wine can be imported and sold through normal commercial channels; however departmental taxes (impuesto al consumo de licores) apply to wine: rates vary by department (approx 8% of commercial value for wine-like products); Bogota DC has its own impuesto al consumo; complex multi-layer tax: national IVA 19% + departmental consumption tax
notes: Wine excluded from distilled spirits monopoly but subject to departmental consumption taxes; tax varies by department; Bogota has separate tax administration; small wine market means enforcement less robust than spirits sector

regulation_name: GMC Resolution 12/02 — regulation harmonisee vin Mercosur
regulation_ref: GMC (Grupo Mercado Comun) Resolucion 12/02 - Reglamento Tecnico Mercosur para Vinos y Bebidas Fermentadas
enforcement_body: CMC (Consejo Mercado Comun) + GMC technical groups
key_requirements: Mercosur harmonised technical standard for wine and fermented beverages; defines wine, sparkling wine, fortified wine, wine vinegar categories; physico-chemical standards (volatile acidity, sulphur dioxide limits, reducing sugars); labelling requirements harmonised across Argentina, Brazil, Paraguay, Uruguay (and Venezuela suspended); mutual recognition of wine controls; reduces non-tariff barriers for intra-Mercosur wine trade; zero tariff on intra-zone wine; Chile as associated member benefits
notes: Mercosur zero tariff on wine = major advantage for AR/CL wine in Brazil market; GMC Resolution 12/02 basis for harmonised labelling; national implementations vary; Uruguay small wine producer (tannat grape); Paraguay = minimal wine production; Argentine Malbec + Chilean red dominate intra-zone trade

regulation_ref: GB 15037-2006 (Vin - specification nationale) + Food Safety Law (Loi n 28/2009 amendee)
enforcement_body: SAMR (State Administration for Market Regulation) + GACC + NHC
key_requirements: China national standard GB 15037 defines wine: product specifications, analytical requirements (alcohol, sugar content, volatile acidity, SO2 limits, heavy metals); labelling per GB 7718 (general food labelling) and GB 10344 (alcohol labelling): product name in Chinese mandatory, alcohol content, net content, producer, production date, best before date, standard code; health warning: excessive drinking is harmful to health in Chinese; sulfite declaration; imported wine: importer registration with GACC mandatory
notes: China = world's 5th largest wine consumer; domestic wine production significant (Ningxia, Xinjiang, Shandong); Australian wine imports banned 2020-2023 (anti-dumping tariffs, now removed); French wine strong market position; GACC importer registration mandatory; Chinese label required (overwrap sticker permitted for imports)

regulation_name: GACC import filing + etiquetage douanier Chine
regulation_ref: GACC Order 249/2021 (food importer registration) + Customs Law + Consumption Tax regulations
enforcement_body: GACC (General Administration of Customs) + State Taxation Administration
key_requirements: All wine importers must register with GACC online system; country-specific import requirements (phytosanitary certificates from origin country); customs duty on wine: MFN rate 14% ad valorem; FTA rates (Australia 0% since FTA, Chile 0%, NZ 0%); Consumption Tax = 10% on wine; VAT 13%; Chinese label mandatory before customs clearance (pre-application process or approved importer adds sticker); CIQ inspection at port; cold chain requirements for premium wine
notes: GACC registration number mandatory on Chinese wine label; MFN 14% + 10% consumption tax + 13% VAT = significant import cost; FTA partners have competitive advantage; European wine anti-dumping investigation monitoring; Chinese consumers increasingly demand certification/authenticity

regulation_name: Dutiable Commodities Ordinance — zero duty wine depuis 2008
regulation_ref: Dutiable Commodities Ordinance (Cap. 109) - wine duty removed February 2008
enforcement_body: Hong Kong Customs and Excise Department + Trade and Industry Department
key_requirements: Hong Kong removed all duty on wine in February 2008 (from 40% previously); zero duty on wine = HK became Asia's premier wine trading hub; no import licence required for wine; food safety requirements under Food Safety Ordinance (Cap. 612) - importer registration required; Food and Environmental Hygiene Department controls; labelling: English and/or Chinese; Hong Kong Competition Ordinance applies to wine distribution; Sotheby's/Christie's wine auctions significant; massive wine re-export to mainland China
notes: HK zero wine duty = major wine hub since 2008; significant re-export to mainland China; wine auction market world-class; bonded wine storage industry; simple import process vs complex mainland China procedures; HK national security law impacts some trade flows but wine trade continues

regulation_name: Tobacco and Alcohol Tax Act + TFDA labeling Taiwan
regulation_ref: Tobacco and Alcohol Tax Act + Food Safety and Sanitation Act + TFDA regulations
enforcement_body: Ministry of Finance + TFDA (Taiwan Food and Drug Administration) + Customs Administration
key_requirements: Taiwan: wine (fermented alcohol 0.5% ABV or more) regulated under Tobacco and Alcohol Tax Act; excise: wine 14% ABV or less = NT 7/litre; wine more than 14% = NT 9/litre; sparkling wine = NT 26/litre; import wine: customs duty 30% MFN (significant); importer licence required; labelling in Traditional Chinese mandatory: product name, alcohol content, volume, country of origin, importer, ingredients, health warning; TFDA food safety inspection; prohibited: sales to minors under 18; advertising restrictions
notes: Taiwan 30% MFN wine duty = significant import cost; government wine import licence system (Ministry of Finance); TFDA food safety; health warning mandatory in Chinese; Taiwan liberalised wine import licensing in recent years; growing premium wine market

regulation_name: Liquor Tax Act (酒税法) + labelling JFRA Japan
regulation_ref: Liquor Tax Act (Sake-zei Hou) + Food Labeling Act (Shokuhin Hyoji Ho) + NTA notifications
enforcement_body: National Tax Agency (NTA) + Consumer Affairs Agency + MAFF
key_requirements: Japan: wine classified under Liquor Tax Act; wine excise: approx 80 JPY/litre; NTA administers liquor licence system; Liquor Licence mandatory for retail; importer requires importer's licence and customs declaration; mandatory labelling (Food Labeling Act): product name in Japanese, volume, alcohol content, country of origin, importer, allergens (sulfites mandatory if 10mg/kg or more), best before or expiry; no mandatory health warning text but recommended; JAS (Japan Agricultural Standards) for wine GI protection
notes: Japan = significant wine import market (France, Italy, Chile top suppliers); liquor retail licence complex; wine sold at convenience stores, supermarkets, speciality stores; NTA e-Tax for alcohol excise; JAS wine GI protection for Japanese wines (Yamanashi GI); Japanese wines (Koshu grape) growing internationally; sake/shochu domestic competition

regulation_name: JAS — Japanese Agricultural Standards for wine GI (Japon)
regulation_ref: JAS Act + NTA GI regulations for alcohol
enforcement_body: MAFF (Ministry of Agriculture Forestry and Fisheries) + NTA
key_requirements: NTA manages GI system for wine and spirits under Liquor Tax Act amendment (2015); Japanese GI for wine: Yamanashi, Nagano, Hokkaido, Yamagata, Osaka designated; must use 100% Japanese grapes from designated area; strict vintage/variety declaration requirements; anti-sounding name protection; JAS voluntary certification adds traceability for export; imported wine cannot claim Japanese GI designations; EU-Japan EPA includes mutual GI recognition
notes: EU-Japan EPA (2019): EU wine GIs protected in Japan (Champagne, Bordeaux etc.); Japanese GIs protected in EU (Yamanashi wine); NTA GI list growing; Japanese Koshu wine gaining Decanter recognition; J-FISH QR code traceability system for Japanese agricultural products

regulation_name: Liquor Tax Act + Food Sanitation Act Korea (Coree)
regulation_ref: Liquor Tax Act (Juse-beop) + Food Sanitation Act (Sikpum-wisenggbeop) + NTS regulations
enforcement_body: NTS (National Tax Service) + Ministry of Food and Drug Safety (MFDS) + Korea Customs Service
key_requirements: Korea: wine excise under Liquor Tax Act = ad valorem 30% on import value + education tax 10% of liquor tax; total effective excise approx 33%; customs duty MFN 15% for wine; Korea-EU FTA = 0% wine tariff (phased to zero by 2014); import clearance via MFDS: safety certification, label review; Korean label mandatory (sticker permitted): product name, alcohol content, country of origin, importer, vintage, grape variety, warnings; health warning mandatory; prohibited sale to minors under 19; no sale after 23:00 in some venues
notes: Korea-EU FTA zero wine tariff = major EU wine advantage; Korea-US FTA also zero; wine market growing rapidly (especially among younger urban consumers); MFDS labelling compliance critical; Korean language label mandatory; soju (domestic spirits) dominant but wine gaining market share

regulation_name: Customs Act + Health Sciences Authority (alcool Singapour)
regulation_ref: Customs Act (Cap. 70) + Sale of Food Act + HSA regulations + Liquor Control (Supply and Consumption) Act 2015
enforcement_body: Singapore Customs + Health Sciences Authority (HSA) + Singapore Food Agency (SFA)
key_requirements: Singapore: wine import requires customs declaration; excise duty on wine = SGD 70/litre of alcohol (effective approx SGD 6.47 per 750ml bottle at 12.3% ABV); importer registration with Singapore Customs mandatory; SFA food safety controls; labelling: English mandatory, allergens, nutrition info not mandatory for wine (exemption); Liquor Control (Supply and Consumption) Act 2015: restricted hours for public drinking (no alcohol in public after 22:30); Liquor Act licence for retail/on-trade; no sale to under-18s; Little India, Boat Quay liquor restriction zones
notes: Singapore excise approx SGD 70/litre of alcohol = significant; wine popular in Singapore (cosmopolitan culture, high income); SGD 6.47 per bottle excise minimum; Singapore major wine import and re-export hub for SEA; stringent anti-drink-driving enforcement (BAC 0.08%)

regulation_name: Food Act 1983 + restrictions halal — alcool restreint (Malaisie)
regulation_ref: Food Act 1983 + Food Regulations 1985 + Customs Duties Order + Syariah law provisions
enforcement_body: Ministry of Health Malaysia + Royal Malaysian Customs + JAKIM (Jabatan Kemajuan Islam Malaysia)
key_requirements: Malaysia: wine sale BANNED for Muslims (Syariah law in all 14 states/territories - shariah criminal offences for Muslims); wine permitted for non-Muslims in licensed premises only; dual regulatory system: civil law for non-Muslims, Syariah for Muslims; Federal excise duty on wine: RM 5.00/litre of product; customs duty MFN approx 25%; import licence required; labelling in Bahasa Malaysia and English; health warning; no halal certification possible for wine; wine sales restricted to non-Muslim-majority areas; Kelantan and Terengganu effectively prohibit all alcohol
notes: CRITICAL: wine is BANNED for Malaysian Muslims (60%+ of population) under Syariah law; civil law permits wine for non-Muslims but with significant restrictions; Kelantan/Terengganu = de facto full prohibition; wine available in supermarkets (non-Muslim areas), hotel bars, Chinese restaurants in peninsular Malaysia; tourist areas (KL, Penang, Langkawi) more accessible

regulation_name: Liquor Act BE 2493 + FDA labelling Thailande
regulation_ref: Liquor Act B.E. 2493 (1950, as amended) + Beverage Labeling Act + FDA regulations
enforcement_body: Excise Department + FDA Thailand + Department of Special Investigation
key_requirements: Thailand: wine classified as liquor under Liquor Act; Excise Department controls production and import; import licence required; excise duty on wine: graduated by CIF value - approximately 54% ad valorem + volume component; customs duty MFN 54% (reduced to 0% under ASEAN AFTA for ASEAN-origin wine - limited wine producers in ASEAN); Thai label mandatory (or Thai sticker); health warnings: alcohol sales prohibited to under-20s; prohibited advertising of alcohol (images of bottles, brand logos restricted in Thai media); time restrictions: 11:00-14:00 and 17:00-24:00 only for alcohol sales
notes: Thailand 54% excise + 54% customs duty = total tax burden very high on imported wine; wine market growing in Bangkok/tourist areas; Temple/religious day (Wan Phra) alcohol sales prohibited; Thai health warning mandatory on bottles; advertising ban strictly enforced

regulation_ref: Decree 105/2017/ND-CP (Management and trading of alcoholic beverages) + Decree 100/2019 (DUI)
enforcement_body: Ministry of Industry and Trade (MOIT) + Ministry of Health + General Department of Vietnam Customs
key_requirements: Vietnam: Decree 105/2017 regulates wine trade; business licence required for wine import/distribution; wholesale licence for companies with warehousing; retail licence for outlets; labels in Vietnamese required; mandatory information: product name, alcohol content, volume, origin, importer, date of manufacture/expiry; health warning in Vietnamese; excise tax (Special Consumption Tax - SCT): wine 35% ad valorem; customs duty MFN varies (approx 30-50%); no sale to under-18s; Decree 100/2019: zero BAC limit for professional drivers
notes: Vietnam wine market growing rapidly; Decree 100/2019 zero BAC professional driving = strict; SCT 35% + customs duty significant import cost; Vietnamese-language label mandatory; wine culture developing in Ho Chi Minh City and Hanoi; Dalat wine (domestic production, limited quality)

regulation_name: PP 74/2013 — restrictions alcool + distribution controlee Indonesia
regulation_ref: Government Regulation (PP) 74/2013 (Control and supervision of alcoholic beverages) + Permendag 20/M-DAG/PER/4/2014
enforcement_body: BPOM (Badan Pengawas Obat dan Makanan) + Kementerian Perdagangan + Customs + Regional governments
key_requirements: Indonesia: alcohol classified by level: Group A (beer max 5%), Group B (5-20% - wine), Group C (more than 20%); wine distribution VERY RESTRICTED: only licensed importers; only sold in certain locations (hotels 5 star minimum, restaurants, special bars, duty free); BANNED from convenience stores/minimarkets/supermarkets since 2015 Permendag restriction; BPOM pre-market approval mandatory; labelling in Indonesian; health warning mandatory; Bali/tourist areas have some accommodation; some provinces and regencies impose complete alcohol bans; Aceh province = full sharia prohibition
notes: Indonesia wine = HIGHLY RESTRICTED despite secular constitution; 2015 Permendag effectively banned supermarket wine sales nationwide; wine only available in 5-star hotels, licensed restaurants, duty-free; Aceh = complete prohibition; Bali = most accessible but still licensed premises only; large Muslim majority population; proposed complete national alcohol ban discussed periodically

regulation_name: BIR excise tax + FDA etiquetage Philippines
regulation_ref: National Internal Revenue Code (NIRC) - excise on alcohol + Food and Drug Administration Act + BFAD circulars
enforcement_body: Bureau of Internal Revenue (BIR) + Philippine FDA + Bureau of Customs
key_requirements: Philippines: wine excise under NIRC; still wine duty = PHP 30/litre (as amended by TRAIN Law 2018); sparkling wine = PHP 630/litre; customs duty MFN 20% on wine; Philippine FDA clearance for imported wine; importer registration; labelling: English/Filipino; mandatory information: product name, ABV, volume, origin, importer, net weight; no sale to under-18; LGU (Local Government Unit) business permits for liquor sales; barangay ordinances may restrict alcohol
notes: Philippines TRAIN Law 2018 restructured alcohol excise; wine market relatively small vs beer; San Miguel Beer dominates domestic market; imported wine growing in premium segments (Manila, Cebu, tourist areas); LGU alcohol ordinances vary widely

regulation_name: Reglementation alcool par Etat — constitution indienne (State List)
regulation_ref: Constitution of India - Seventh Schedule, List II, Entry 8 (State List - intoxicating liquors) + FSSAI Food Safety
enforcement_body: State Excise Departments (29 states) + FSSAI + Central Customs
key_requirements: India: alcohol regulation is ENTIRELY STATE SUBJECT under Indian constitution - massive variation; prohibition states: Gujarat, Bihar, Nagaland, Manipur (complete prohibition); high-tax states: Telangana, Tamil Nadu; TASMAC (Tamil Nadu) = state monopoly; partial prohibition: some districts; liquor import: central customs duty on wine = 150% (basic customs duty) + IGST 18-21%; state entry permits required; FSSAI labelling standards applicable nationally; FSSAI licence mandatory for import and sale; label: FSSAI logo, nutritional info per serving, health warning
notes: CRITICAL diversity: Gujarat (200M+ pop) = complete prohibition; Maharashtra (Mumbai) = liberal; Delhi = relatively accessible; 150% basic customs duty = imported wine very expensive; FSSAI state-level licences complex; India wine production growing (Nashik region - Sula Vineyards); imported wine niche premium market; state-by-state entry permits required for inter-state movement

regulation_name: Wine Australia Act 2013 — production et export (Australie)
regulation_ref: Wine Australia Act 2013 (formerly Australian Wine and Brandy Corporation Act 1980) + FSANZ Standard 2.7.4
enforcement_body: Wine Australia (statutory authority) + FSANZ (Food Standards Australia New Zealand) + ATO
key_requirements: Australia: Wine Australia manages GI (Geographical Indication) system, export licensing, and wine research; Geographic Indications: Barossa Valley, Coonawarra, Margaret River etc. legally protected; export licence mandatory for all Australian wine exports; wine must meet FSANZ Standard 2.7.4 for composition, labelling; Food Standards Code: must declare alcohol content (% vol), standard drinks per container, pregnancy warning icon (mandatory since 31 July 2023 for wine), allergens (sulfites); wine must state Country of Origin; Australian wine excise: free-range threshold under WET rebate scheme
notes: Australia mandatory pregnancy pictogram (crossed glass symbol) mandatory from July 2023 on wine labels; Australia-China trade tension 2020-2023 (anti-dumping tariffs on Australian wine removed April 2024); Wine Equalisation Tax (WET) 29% applies to wine at wholesale level; WET rebate for small producers; FSANZ food standards apply in both AU and NZ

regulation_name: Wine Equalisation Tax (WET) + Excise Tariff Act Australie
regulation_ref: A New Tax System (Wine Equalisation Tax) Act 1999 + Excise Tariff Act 1921
key_requirements: WET: 29% of wholesale value of wine; applies to wine (including fruit wine, mead); WET rebate: small wine producers (up to AUD 350,000 WET in sales) receive 29% rebate up to AUD 350,000 cap; imported wine not eligible for WET rebate; GST 10% also applies; import wine: customs duty (varies by country - FTA rates for NZ, US, Chile, UK, EU) + WET + GST; Australia-EU FTA (approved 2024) = zero tariff on EU wine; total tax on imported wine still significant (WET + GST)
notes: WET = 29% of wholesale value - applies wholesale level; retail wine price includes WET embedded; small producers benefit from rebate scheme (competitive vs large); Australia-EU FTA 2024 = zero tariff but WET still applies to imports; Australian wine industry lobbying for WET reform

regulation_ref: Wine Act 2003 (NZ) + Sale and Supply of Alcohol Act 2012 (SSAA) + FSANZ Standard 2.7.4
enforcement_body: NZ Food Safety (NZFSA) + Wine Standards Management Plan (WSMP) + ARLA (Alcohol Regulatory and Licensing Authority)
key_requirements: New Zealand: Wine Act 2003 requires all NZ wineries to have Wine Standards Management Plan; FSANZ Standard 2.7.4 applies (same as Australia); Marlborough Sauvignon Blanc GI informal but major commercial GI; Waipara, Hawke's Bay, Central Otago GIs developing; SSAA 2012 regulates retail/on-trade licence; purchase age 18+; alcohol advertising restricted (voluntary Code + Broadcasting Standards Authority); mandatory label: alcohol content, standard drinks, pregnancy warning (same as Australia mandatory pictogram from 2023); import licence for wine not required (food importer registration)
notes: NZ wine = Marlborough Sauvignon Blanc globally dominant; CER with Australia = free trade in wine; NZ exports primarily to UK, USA, Australia; NZ wines exempt from WET in Australia (CER); FSANZ Standard 2.7.4 applies identically; NZ pregnancy warning = mandatory same timeline as Australia

regulation_name: ASEAN AFTA — alcool exclu des engagements communs
regulation_ref: ASEAN Trade in Goods Agreement (ATIGA) + ASEAN Framework Agreement on Services
key_requirements: ASEAN free trade area generally excludes alcohol from tariff elimination commitments (sensitive list/highly sensitive list); wine remains subject to national excise and high import duties in most ASEAN members despite AFTA; however some intra-ASEAN harmonisation of food labelling; Singapore food labelling standards referenced across region; ASEAN-wide there is no harmonised alcohol regulation framework; individual member states maintain full sovereignty over alcohol policy; ASEAN Harmonised Tariff Nomenclature (AHTN) Chapter 22 for wine
notes: ASEAN has no harmonised alcohol framework; each member maintains distinct regulatory regime; wine generally = sensitive list item excluded from AFTA tariff preferences (Malaysia, Indonesia, Thailand, Vietnam, Philippines each maintain high wine tariffs + own import regimes); Singapore most liberal; Myanmar additional post-coup complexity

regulation_name: Alcool BANNI Arabie Saoudite + Koweit — reglemente UAE/Bahrein
regulation_ref: National laws: KSA Royal Decree M/8 (prohibition); Kuwait Law 24/1964; UAE Federal Law No. 3/1987 + Abu Dhabi Alcohol Regulation; Bahrain Municipal regulations; Qatar Alcohol regulations with Tourism Hotel licence exception
enforcement_body: Ministries of Interior + Tourism authorities + Religious authorities
key_requirements: GCC alcohol regime highly variable: Saudi Arabia: TOTAL BAN on alcohol - possession and consumption illegal for everyone (Muslim and non-Muslim); Kuwait: TOTAL BAN; Qatar: alcohol restricted to licensed hotel venues and Qatar Distribution Company (QDC) for non-Muslims; UAE: permitted for non-Muslims in licensed venues; Abu Dhabi Alcohol Regulation allows sale in licensed venues/tourist areas; Bahrain: most liberal GCC state - alcohol available in licensed retail and on-trade; Oman: alcohol for non-Muslims in licensed venues; import of wine to Saudi Arabia/Kuwait = illegal/seized at customs
